title: Diagnosing Forgetting in Production
concept: Always evaluate fine-tuned models on a general benchmark (like MMLU) alongside your task-specific test set to catch regressions. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Model Merging & Soups.'

exercises.prompt: What is a common risk when you fine-tune an AI model on a very deeply on a single specific task?

options.text: It might lose some of its general knowledge.
It automatically gets better at all tasks.

exercises.explain: Fine-tuning teaches a model new tricks, but it can accidentally overwrite older, general knowledge. This is called forgetting.

cards.title: Why Did My Model Forget?
cards.body: Your newly fine-tuned model is amazing at medical jargon, but it just failed a basic middle-school math question! Why does this happen, and how do we spot it before our users do? Let's learn how to diagnose forgetting in production.

cards.title: Diagnosing Forgetting
cards.body: When we fine-tune a model on a specific task, it can experience 'catastrophic forgetting' where it loses its general knowledge. To diagnose this, we must run evaluations on both our custom task-specific dataset and a general benchmark (like MMLU) to detect performance drops.

cards.title: The Specialized Chef
cards.body: Imagine training a professional chef to bake the ultimate chocolate cake. You spend weeks perfecting their cake skills. But when you ask them to make a simple salad, they've completely forgotten how to chop lettuce! By keeping a standard cookbook (a general benchmark) on the counter, you can test them periodically to make sure their basic kitchen skills don't disappear while they specialize.

cards.title: The Forgotten English Case
cards.body: A team at a startup fine-tuned a model to write python code. It got 95% on their coding test. However, when they ran it against MMLU (a general knowledge benchmark), its score dropped from 70% to 30%. The model forgot how to write basic English! They caught this early because they ran both evaluations side-by-side.

cards.title: The Perfect Score Trap
cards.body: Myth: If my model gets a perfect score on my new dataset, it is 100% ready for production. Reality: It might have gotten 'smarter' at your task by wiping out its general reasoning abilities. You must test both!

cards.title: The Evaluation Dashboard
cards.body: Comparing a healthy specialized model vs one suffering from catastrophic forgetting.

right.text: High score on specialized task BUT terrible score on general benchmarks.

left.text: High score on specialized task AND high score on general benchmarks.

exercises.prompt: Why is it important to evaluate your fine-tuned model on a general benchmark alongside your custom test set?

options.text: It makes the model train much faster.
It helps catch regressions and loss of general knowledge.
It automatically fixes formatting bugs in your dataset.
It guarantees the model will never make a mistake again.

exercises.explain: Evaluating a model on general benchmarks like MMLU helps you spot 'catastrophic forgetting' where the model loses its broad abilities while learning a specific task.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If your model gets a high score on your specific dataset, you do not need to test it on general benchmarks.
exercises.explain: Even if a model becomes an expert at your target task, it can still suffer from catastrophic forgetting and lose basic reasoning skills.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the name of the general benchmark used to check for regressions:
exercises.sentence: To make sure your model still understands general knowledge, you should test it on MMLU alongside your task-specific test.
exercises.explain: A general benchmark like MMLU helps you verify that your model hasn't lost its broad reasoning and general knowledge skills during fine-tuning.

exercises.prompt: A base model started with 70% on MMLU. Which model shows clear signs of catastrophic forgetting after being fine-tuned for SQL?
exercises.explain: Model B shows typical signs of catastrophic forgetting: it got better at the new task (SQL) but crashed on general knowledge (MMLU).

exercises.prompt: You are deploying a newly fine-tuned medical chatbot. How should you evaluate it to ensure it hasn't lost its general reasoning skills?

options.text: Only test it on the new medical dataset. If it scores 100%, it is ready for production.
Stop using fine-tuning completely and write longer prompts instead.
Evaluate it on both your medical dataset and a general benchmark like MMLU.
Deploy it immediately and wait for user complaints to find bugs.

exercises.explain: To prevent your model from losing its mind on general knowledge while specializing, always test it against a general benchmark alongside your specific dataset. Next up: Model Merging & Soups.
